refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 requires quantification of the availability target and clarification of measurement scope. Link availability is a system-level quality attribute that depends on network infrastructure, redundancy mechanisms, and fault detection/recovery. The refined FR explicitly defines the metric (availability ratio), threshold (≥0.999), and measurement period to enable objective verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculation of cumulative downtime; comparison against 0.999 threshold. Requires instrumentation of link health status and timestamped event logging.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures within [X] seconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate failover or graceful degradation upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall log link state events with timestamps for availability calculation.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The network infrastructure shall provide redundant communication paths or recovery mechanisms to support ≥0.999 availability.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual-path network architecture (primary + backup link) with automatic failover and health monitoring at the Teleoperation module.
DP-B: Implement network redundancy and link health monitoring distributed across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules with coordinated failover logic.
DP-C: Implement centralized link availability management service with real-time monitoring, failover orchestration, and telemetry aggregation.
DP-D: Implement link availability through vendor-managed network service (e.g., cellular carrier SLA) with contractual guarantees and monitoring integration.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement dual-path network architecture (primary + backup link) with automatic failover and health monitoring at the Teleoperation module. This DP provides deterministic control over link redundancy and failover logic, aligns with safety-critical requirements (high criticality), and concentrates availability logic in a single architectural boundary (Teleoperation) to minimize coupling and diffusion.

potential_sources: Coupling to Vehicle module: Vehicle must report operational status and accept safe-state commands during link loss (mitigated by well-defined SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces).
Coupling to Safety Services: Failover and degradation decisions must respect safety policies (mitigated by SafetyAlert interface and pre-defined safe states).
Coupling to Network Link interface: Dual-path implementation depends on availability of two independent network paths (assumption: infrastructure provides this).
coupling_check.notes: DP-A exhibits low coupling because failover logic is encapsulated within Teleoperation; external modules interact only through standard interfaces (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Telemetry). Dual-path redundancy is a well-established pattern with minimal cross-module dependencies.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Two independent network paths (e.g., LTE + satellite, or dual cellular carriers) are available at the worksite.
ASSUMPTION: Link health can be monitored via heartbeat/keepalive messages without excessive overhead.
RISK: If both paths fail simultaneously (common-mode failure), availability target cannot be met; mitigation requires diversity in network providers or technologies.
RISK: Failover latency may exceed safety-critical response time requirements; must be validated against FR-NGHE-010.1 (detection time).
RISK: Operator experience during failover (e.g., brief command lag) may impact usability; requires human factors validation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly specify: (1) failover detection mechanism (e.g., heartbeat timeout threshold), (2) failover latency budget (e.g., <500ms), (3) safe-state behavior during transition, and (4) network path diversity requirements (e.g., different carriers, technologies). Consider adding a tertiary path (e.g., local autonomous mode) for ultra-high availability scenarios.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the dual-path network architecture, link health monitoring, failover logic, and command/telemetry routing. It interfaces with Vehicle (via Tasking and SafetyAlert) and external network infrastructure (via Network Link). It does NOT own the physical network infrastructure or Vehicle control logic.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with primary and backup network paths; Teleoperation module monitors link health and selects active path based on availability.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation sends operator commands to Vehicle; during link loss, Tasking is suspended or replaced with safe-state commands (e.g., stop, hold position).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation receives vehicle state, sensor data, and link health status from Vehicle; used for operator situational awareness and failover decision-making.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation receives safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services; sends link-loss alerts to Safety Services to trigger safe-state enforcement on Vehicle.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. Dual-path failover, health monitoring, and command routing are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces cross module boundaries, both well-defined.

Vehicle could implement link monitoring, but this would couple network logic to vehicle control, violating separation of concerns. Vehicle should only respond to link-loss alerts via SafetyAlert, not manage failover.
Safety Services should enforce safe-state policies, not manage network failover. Placing link availability logic in Safety Services would conflate network resilience with safety enforcement.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container; link availability is a subsystem concern best owned by Teleoperation. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and obscure architectural boundaries.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns dual-path network architecture, link health monitoring, and failover orchestration. Vehicle and Safety Services are consumers of link-state information via SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces. This assignment maximizes cohesion (all link-availability logic in one module) and minimizes coupling (external modules interact only through standard interfaces).
containment_risks: RISK: If network infrastructure is shared with other systems (e.g., site-wide WiFi), Teleoperation cannot guarantee availability without infrastructure-level SLAs; mitigation requires contractual agreements with network provider.

RISK: Failover logic may require Vehicle-specific knowledge (e.g., safe-state commands); if Vehicle interface is poorly defined, failover may be ineffective; mitigation requires detailed Tasking and SafetyAlert interface specifications.
RISK: Dual-path implementation assumes two independent network paths are available; if worksite provides only one path, DP-A is infeasible; mitigation requires early infrastructure assessment.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has exclusive ownership of link health monitoring and failover logic. Define clear boundaries: Teleoperation manages network-layer redundancy; Vehicle manages safe-state transitions; Safety Services manages policy enforcement. Document assumptions about network infrastructure (dual paths, carrier diversity) in the module specification.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (dual-path failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (link availability ≥0.999). The DP is specific, measurable, and technically feasible. Coupling analysis shows low cross-module dependencies. Assumptions (dual paths, heartbeat monitoring) are explicit and can be validated. DP-A is recommended for advancement.

diffusion_risks: Failover logic must coordinate with Vehicle safe-state behavior; SafetyAlert interface must be robust and latency-bounded.
Network Link interface must support dual-path abstraction; if underlying infrastructure does not provide independent paths, DP-A cannot be realized.
Telemetry interface must include link health status; if Vehicle does not report link metrics, Teleoperation cannot make informed failover decisions.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the clear owner (containment_score 0.95). All link-availability logic is contained within Teleoperation; external modules interact only through well-defined interfaces. No diffusion of failover logic across Vehicle, Safety Services, or NGHE Solution.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via continuous monitoring of link state and availability calculation. Requires instrumentation of Network Link interface and timestamped event logging. Acceptance criteria (≥0.999 availability) is objective and measurable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns link availability; Vehicle owns safe-state transitions; Safety Services owns policy enforcement. Interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and support verification.

recommended_revisions: Refine FR-NGHE-010.1 (detection time) and FR-NGHE-010.2 (failover latency) to quantify acceptable response times; these are critical for safety-critical operations.
Specify Network Link interface in detail: dual-path abstraction, health monitoring protocol, failover signaling mechanism.
Define safe-state behavior during link loss: e.g., Vehicle enters hold-position mode, operator receives visual/audio alert, tele-operator can switch to local autonomous mode.
Document assumptions about network infrastructure: dual paths, carrier diversity, SLA guarantees.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (detection, failover, logging, infrastructure). DP-A is sound and well-contained in Teleoperation, but further refinement is needed to address sub-FRs and validate assumptions. Recommend decomposing to the next level to detail failover logic, safe-state transitions, and network infrastructure requirements.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures within [X] seconds (e.g., 5 seconds) via heartbeat timeout or explicit link-down signal.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate failover to backup path within [Y] seconds (e.g., 2 seconds) of detecting primary link failure.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall enforce safe-state behavior (e.g., stop, hold position) during link loss and notify Safety Services via SafetyAlert.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions (up/down, failover events) with timestamps for post-evaluation availability calculation.
FR-NGHE-010.5: The network infrastructure shall provide two independent communication paths (e.g., LTE + satellite, or dual cellular carriers) with <[Z]% correlation in failure modes.

assumptions: Two independent network paths are available at the worksite (e.g., LTE + satellite, or dual cellular carriers).
Link health can be monitored via heartbeat/keepalive messages without excessive latency or bandwidth overhead.
Vehicle can transition to safe-state (stop, hold position) within acceptable latency (e.g., <1 second) upon receiving SafetyAlert.
Operator can tolerate brief command lag (<500ms) during failover without safety impact.
Network infrastructure provides SLA guarantees or contractual availability commitments.
open_risks: Common-mode failure: If both network paths fail simultaneously (e.g., due to site-wide power outage or natural disaster), availability target cannot be met. Mitigation: tertiary path (e.g., local autonomous mode) or acceptance of residual risk.

Failover latency: If failover detection + execution exceeds safety-critical response time, operator may lose control during critical maneuvers. Mitigation: quantify latency budget and validate via testing.
Network infrastructure dependency: If worksite provides only one network path, DP-A is infeasible. Mitigation: early infrastructure assessment and contingency planning.
Operator experience: Failover may cause brief command lag or telemetry gaps, impacting operator situational awareness. Mitigation: human factors testing and operator training.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure assessment: confirm dual-path availability, carrier diversity, and SLA guarantees.
Failover latency measurement: quantify detection time, failover execution time, and total latency under various failure scenarios.
Safe-state validation: confirm Vehicle can transition to safe-state within acceptable latency and that SafetyAlert interface is robust.
Availability calculation methodology: define measurement period, downtime accounting (e.g., planned maintenance exclusions), and acceptance criteria.
Operator usability testing: validate that failover behavior does not degrade operator situational awareness or safety.
